Good progress. Three real-world friction points to call out and clear up:

## What I see in your output

- вң… GPU + driver + Ollama-on-GPU all working вҖ” that's the part that matters
- вң… `mxbai-embed-large` pulled successfully
- вқҢ PyTorch is the CPU-only wheel (the reranker is silently running on CPU right now, which is why startup feels sluggish)
- вҡ пёҸ You're in **CMD**, not PowerShell вҖ” that's why `#` and `Remove-Item` errored. No big deal, just need CMD-syntax commands.

## Run these (CMD-friendly, copy-paste one at a time)

```bat
:: 1. Wipe the old chroma_db (CMD syntax вҖ” not Remove-Item)
rmdir /s /q C:\Users\LocalLarry\Documents\LocalLarry\chroma_db

:: 2. Move PyTorch to the CUDA build (this is the only real FAIL)
pip uninstall -y torch torchvision torchaudio
pip install torch torchvision torchaudio --index-url https://download.pytorch.org/whl/cu124

:: 3. Verify it took
python -c "import torch; print('torch:', torch.__version__, '| cuda:', torch.cuda.is_available(), '| device:', torch.cuda.get_device_nam

Then disable what you find:

Scheduled task вҶ’ Disable-ScheduledTask -TaskName "<name>" -TaskPath "<path>". Important: also open it in Task Scheduler and check the Settings tab вҶ’ uncheck "If the task fails, restartвҖҰ", and review Triggers. The restart setting is the usual culprit for the End-Task respawn.
Run-key entry вҶ’ delete the value, or just uncheck it in Task Manager вҶ’ Startup tab.
Startup-folder shortcut вҶ’ delete it.
Watchdog parent (from Process Explorer) вҶ’ kill/disable that process and find its autostart entry the same way.

The fastest single tool for the whole picture is Sysinternals Autoruns вҖ” its Logon, Scheduled Tasks, and Services tabs show every persistence point at once; filter for terminal/ftmo and untick.

в—Ҹ You have an RTX 4060 running at 60Hz вҖ” let me walk you through everything to get 144Hz and optimize for Fortnite.

  ---
  Step 1 вҖ” Enable 144Hz in Windows

  1. Right-click your Desktop вҶ’ Display settings
  2. Scroll down вҶ’ Advanced display settings
  3. Click Display adapter properties for Display 1
  4. Go to the Monitor tab
  5. Under Screen refresh rate вҶ’ select 144 Hertz
  6. Click Apply вҶ’ Keep changes

# Larry G-Force v2.1 - Clean Production Distribution

This folder contains the clean, production-ready version of the Larry G-Force agent system.

**Skills Philosophy**: Skills are converted into high-quality, fully local Python + Ollama executable MCP variants. No external skill marketplaces. Everything runs on your machine with maximum control and privacy.

## Folder Structure (Professional Framework)

```
GITHUB/
в”ңв”Җв”Җ src/                    # Main editable source (agent, managers, etc.)
в”ңв”Җв”Җ config/                 # All configuration (mcp.json, larry_config.json, etc.)
в”ңв”Җв”Җ prompts/                # System prompts and agent personality
в”ңв”Җв”Җ skills/                 # Local Python + Ollama MCP skill implementations
в”ңв”Җв”Җ mcp/                    # MCP client + servers (including fxjefe-local-mcp)
в”ңв”Җв”Җ core/                   # Core logic (if split from src)
в”ңв”Җв”Җ tools/                  # Tool wrappers (kali, web, etc.)
в”ңв”Җв”Җ api/                    # API layer
в”ңв”Җв”Җ scripts/

2.content:
# Management & activation scripts
в”ңв”Җв”Җ logs/                   # Logging framework
в”ңв”Җв”Җ db/                     # Databases
в”ңв”Җв”Җ data/                   # User data, RAG, sandbox, etc.
в”ңв”Җв”Җ docs/                   # Documentation
в””в”Җв”Җ build_locked.py         # Build script for protected executable version
```

## Two Versions

### 1. Editable / Original Version (Recommended for development)
- Located in `src/`
- Fully readable and modifiable.
- Use with `python manage_larry.py ...` or the launchers.

### 2. Locked / Encrypted Version (For distribution / protection)
- Built using `build_locked.py`
- Uses **PyInstaller** (single .exe) + **PyArmor** (code obfuscation + encryption).
- Protects your intellectual property against casual reverse engineering or theft.
- All user-specific settings live in an external `user_config.json` next to the `.exe`.
  - This allows the same executable to be used on multiple PCs with different accounts/tokens.

**To build the locked version:**
```powershell
cd G

4.content:
This folder contains the clean, production-ready version of the Larry G-Force agent system.

**Skills Philosophy**: Skills are converted into high-quality, fully local Python + Ollama executable MCP variants. No external skill marketplaces. Everything runs on your machine with maximum control and privacy.

## Folder Structure (Professional Framework)

```
GITHUB/
в”ңв”Җв”Җ src/                    # Main editable source (agent, managers, etc.)
в”ңв”Җв”Җ config/                 # All configuration (mcp.json, larry_config.json, etc.)
в”ңв”Җв”Җ prompts/                # System prompts and agent personality
в”ңв”Җв”Җ skills/                 # Local Python + Ollama MCP skill implementations
в”ңв”Җв”Җ mcp/                    # MCP client + servers (including fxjefe-local-mcp)
в”ңв”Җв”Җ core/                   # Core logic (if split from src)
в”ңв”Җв”Җ tools/                  # Tool wrappers (kali, web, etc.)
в”ңв”Җв”Җ api/                    # API layer
в”ңв”Җв”Җ scripts/                # Management & activation scripts
в”ңв”Җв”Җ

5.content:
logs/                   # Logging framework
в”ңв”Җв”Җ db/                     # Databases
в”ңв”Җв”Җ data/                   # User data, RAG, sandbox, etc.
в”ңв”Җв”Җ docs/                   # Documentation
в””в”Җв”Җ build_locked.py         # Build script for protected executable version
```

## Two Versions

### 1. Editable / Original Version (Recommended for development)
- Located in `src/`
- Fully readable and modifiable.
- Use with `python manage_larry.py ...` or the launchers.

### 2. Locked / Encrypted Version (For distribution / protection)
- Built using `build_locked.py`
- Uses **PyInstaller** (single .exe) + **PyArmor** (code obfuscation + encryption).
- Protects your intellectual property against casual reverse engineering or theft.
- All user-specific settings live in an external `user_config.json` next to the `.exe`.
  - This allows the same executable to be used on multiple PCs with different accounts/tokens.

**To build the locked version:**
```powershell
cd GITHUB
python build_locked.py
```

f unloading happened.
        """
        status = self.hardware.get_gpu_status()
        if not status.get("available"):
            return False

        if status["vram_percent_used"] > threshold_percent:
            print(f"[ModelManager] VRAM pressure high ({status['vram_percent_used']}%). Unloading {heavy_model}...")
            success = self.unload_model(heavy_model)
            time.sleep(2)  # Give Ollama time to release memory
            return success
        return False

    def ensure_model_loaded(self, model_name: str) -> bool:
        """Simple check - in future we can expand this."""
        # For now we just track it
        if model_name not in self.loaded_models:
            self.loaded_models.append(model_name)
        return True

# Global instance
_model_manager: Optional[ModelManager] = None

def get_model_manager() -> ModelManager:
    global _model_manager
    if _model_manager is None:
        _model_manager = ModelManager()

18,376 bytes), and the import succeeded. We're good. I have the canonical version from earlier.

Half the .execute() calls are not bugs
I just looked at the session_manager.py you uploaded:

python
# Line 464:
class TerminalHandler:
    ...
    # Line 500:
    def execute(self, command: str, timeout: int = TERMINAL_TIMEOUT) -> Dict:
TerminalHandler defines its own .execute(). So:

session_manager.py:617, 626, 627 вҖ” self.execute(...) (called inside TerminalHandler) вҶ’ fine
session_manager.py:681 вҖ” self.terminal.execute(args) where self.terminal = TerminalHandler(...) вҶ’ fine
session_manager.py:716 вҖ” th.execute(...) where th = TerminalHandler(...) вҶ’ fine
Five of the eight .execute( calls were noise. Strike them off the list.

What's actually broken вҖ” two fixes
Fix #1: config loader (the current crash)
The MCP loader expects servers as a dict; your mcp.json has it as a list-of-objects with "name" keys. Cleanest fix is to make the loader accept both formats вҖ” that way no Anthropic-flavored JSON rewrite, and the lis

display: olkit.db" -Force# Start Docker Desktop againHeads up: if you had MCP servers configured inside Docker Desktop's MCP Toolkit panel (separate from your Claude Desktop MCP setup), they'll be gone. Your .bak lets you restore if needed. Your regular containers, images, volumes, and WSL distros are untouched вҖ” none of that lives in this DB.Why it ballooned: the MCP Toolkit DB is SQLite and tends to grow with log/telemetry rows even when you're not actively using it. 1.content:
# Running Uncensored Qwen3.6-35B-A3B (and Dense Qwen3.6-27B) via Ollama on Windows for Agentic Coding вҖ” Tuned Setup Plan

## TL;DR
- **Pull the MoE model `huihui_ai/Qwen3.6-abliterated:35b` (24GB, Q4_K_M, text+tools, tool-calling intact) as your primary agentic-coding driver**, or the vision-patched `fredrezones55/Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-Uncensored-HauhauCS-Aggressive:Q4` (22GB); keep the dense `huihui_ai/Qwen3.6-abliterated:27b` (17GB) as a steadier-but-slower fallback. On your RTX 4060 8GB + 64GB DDR5 rig, the 35B-A3B MoE is the right choice precisely because only ~3B of 35B params are active per token, so offloading its experts to RAM costs far less speed than offloading a dense model would.
- **Expect ~30вҖ“35 tok/s generation at modest context**, dropping toward ~15вҖ“29 tok/s at very large context вҖ” corroborated across multiple 8GB-VRAM + 64GB-RAM rigs (and a measured `llama-bench` run on Qwen3.6-35B-A3B UD-Q4_K_M at **35.20 tok/s gen / 268 tok/s prefill**). Set these Windows user environment variables (via

2.content:
System Properties вҶ’ Environment Variables or `setx`), then **fully restart the Ollama app**: `OLLAMA_FLASH_ATTENTION=1`, `OLLAMA_KV_CACHE_TYPE=q8_0`, `OLLAMA_CONTEXT_LENGTH=32768`, `OLLAMA_KEEP_ALIVE=-1`. `OLLAMA_MAX_VRAM` is **not** a working variable вҖ” ignore that advice.
- **Use a custom Modelfile** baking in `PARAMETER num_ctx 32768`, `PARAMETER num_gpu <tuned>`, and the Qwen coding sampler settings, then tune `num_gpu` down from a starting guess while watching VRAM in Task Manager / `nvidia-smi`. The sweet spot for agentic coding is **32K context** (q8_0 KV cache + flash attention); go to 64K only if a task needs it.

## Key Findings

**1. The model is real, current, and tool-capable.** `fredrezones55/Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-Uncensored-HauhauCS-Aggressive` exists on ollama.com, was updated ~6 days ago, has ~10.9K downloads, advertises "0/465 Refusals," and is tagged `vision`, `tools`, `thinking` with a 256K context window. The base is Qwen/Qwen3.6-35B-A3B: 35B total / ~3B active, 256 experts with 8 routed p

3.content:
er token, 40 layers, hybrid linear+full attention (3:1), 262,144 native context. The note "even tool calling at 2-bit quant is strong" traces to the unsloth GGUF discussion; independent quant testing finds tool-calling among the most quantization-resilient categories (KL divergence ~0.069вҖ“0.078 at Q8_0).

**2. Two uncensored families to choose from.** The HauhauCS/fredrezones55 builds are imatrix-quantized and vision-patched. The huihui_ai abliterated builds are a cleaner, more conventional choice for a coding agent: `huihui_ai/Qwen3.6-abliterated:35b` (24GB, the MoE) and `:27b` (17GB, the dense model, Q4_K_M, arch `qwen35`, 27.8B params).

**3. MoE + CPU offload is the whole trick.** With only ~3B active params per token, the optimal strategy is to keep attention/shared/KV on the GPU and push the big, rarely-active expert FFN tensors to system RAM. In llama.cpp this is `-ngl 99 --n-cpu-moe N` (or `-ot "ffn_*_exps=CPU"`). Ollama does not expose `--n-cpu-moe`; it does this automatically via its scheduler, an

4.content:
d you steer it with `num_gpu`.

**4. `OLLAMA_MAX_VRAM` is dead.** Per GitHub issue #5754 ("OLLAMA_MAX_VRAM is ignored"): "I set the OLLAMA_MAX_VRAM env var. I see it is correctly parsed in the logs, but the limit itself is ignored... Even when I set it to an absurdly low value like 5 it still uses more than 6GB." It was deprecated because "it was a hack" and `OLLAMA_GPU_OVERHEAD` "should be used instead." Setting `OLLAMA_MAX_VRAM=64000000000` does nothing. There is no `/etc/ollama/ollama.env` and no `systemctl` on Windows вҖ” those are Linux-only.

## Details

### 1. Uncensored Qwen3.6-35B-A3B GGUF variants вҖ” exact tags and sizes

**On Ollama (one-line `ollama pull` / `ollama run` ready):**

| Tag | Size | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| `fredrezones55/Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-Uncensored-HauhauCS-Aggressive:latest` (=`:Q4`) | 22GB | Q4_K_M-class, vision+tools+thinking, 256K |
| `fredrezones55/Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-Uncensored-HauhauCS-Aggressive:IQ4_XS` | 20GB | smaller 4-bit |
| `fredrezones55/Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-Uncensored-HauhauCS-

5.content:
Aggressive:Q2_K_P` | 16GB | custom "perfect" 2-bit |
| `fredrezones55/Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-Uncensored-HauhauCS-Aggressive:IQ2_M` | 13GB | smallest |
| `huihui_ai/Qwen3.6-abliterated:35b` | 24GB | MoE, abliterated, tools+vision+thinking |
| `huihui_ai/Qwen3.6-abliterated:27b` | 17GB | dense, Q4_K_M, 27.8B |

**On HuggingFace (`unsloth/Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-GGUF`, the SOTA-quality reference):** UD-IQ1_M 10GB, UD-IQ2_XXS 10.8GB, UD-Q2_K_XL 12.3GB, UD-IQ3_XXS 13.2GB, UD-Q3_K_XL 16.8GB, UD-IQ4_XS 17.7GB, UD-Q4_K_M 22.1GB, UD-Q4_K_XL 22.4GB, UD-Q5_K_XL 26.6GB, Q8_0 36.9GB, plus mmproj files (F16 899MB, F32 1.79GB). Per Unsloth's docs (unsloth.ai/docs/models/qwen3.6), "Unsloth ranks first in 21 of 22 model sizes on mean KL divergence" вҖ” KLD measures how closely the quantized model matches the original BF16 output distribution.

The HauhauCS download table (full-precision GGUFs on HF, for llama.cpp): Q8_K_P 44GB, Q6_K_P 31GB, Q5_K_P 28GB, Q4_K_P 23GB, Q4_K_M 21GB, IQ4_NL 20GB, IQ4_XS 19GB, Q3_K_P 19GB, IQ3_M 15GB, Q2_K_P 15GB

6.content:
, IQ2_M 11GB, mmproj-f16 899MB.

**Best quality/size tradeoff for agentic coding:** **Q4_K_M (вүҲ21вҖ“24GB)** is the sweet spot вҖ” the universally-recommended balance of quality, speed, and memory for coding. On your 64GB RAM this fits comfortably with the experts in RAM. IQ4_XS (~19вҖ“20GB) is a slightly smaller fallback. Avoid Q3 and below for agentic/tool-calling work: one practitioner notes "IQ3_XS is fine for autocomplete and single-file work, not for agentic coding... expect the quality gap versus Q4 to show up as weaker tool-call formatting." Q5_K_M/Q6 give marginal quality for materially more RAM and slower expert transfer.

**Vision / mmproj:** The model is natively multimodal, but vision needs a separate `mmproj.gguf` and Ollama's multimodal integration for these patched builds is rough. **For a coding agent, run text-only** вҖ” drop the mmproj entirely. Coding agents don't need the vision tower, and it only adds memory and fragility.

### 2. Exact Ollama configuration on Windows

**How to set envi

7.content:
ronment variables on Windows (NOT Linux):**
- GUI: Quit Ollama from the taskbar tray. Open Settings (Win 11) / Control Panel (Win 10) вҶ’ search "environment variables" вҶ’ "Edit environment variables for your account" вҶ’ add each variable under your **user** account вҶ’ OK вҶ’ relaunch Ollama from the Start menu.
- CMD one-liners (`setx`, persistent, paste one at a time): `setx` writes to the registry and **only affects new sessions** вҖ” you must fully quit and relaunch the Ollama tray app after.
- There is **no `/etc/ollama/ollama.env` and no `systemctl`** on Windows. Those instructions in the FAQ are macOS (`launchctl`) and Linux (`systemctl`) only.

**The variables that matter for you (paste each line in CMD, then restart Ollama):**
```
setx OLLAMA_FLASH_ATTENTION 1
setx OLLAMA_KV_CACHE_TYPE q8_0
setx OLLAMA_CONTEXT_LENGTH 32768
setx OLLAMA_KEEP_ALIVE -1
setx OLLAMA_MAX_LOADED_MODELS 1
setx OLLAMA_NUM_PARALLEL 1
```
- `OLLAMA_FLASH_ATTENTION=1` вҖ” reduces KV-cache memory and is a **prerequisite** for K

8.content:
V cache quantization. Confirmed in Ollama's source (`envconfig`).
- `OLLAMA_KV_CACHE_TYPE=q8_0` вҖ” halves KV memory vs f16 with negligible quality loss. Per Sam McLeod (smcleod.net, who wrote the Ollama KV-quant PR): "Q8_0 вҖ” Minimal quality impact for normal text generation models... Perplexity measurements on an early implementation showed it added around 0.002-0.05~ perplexity." Only takes effect with flash attention on. `q4_0` "cuts it down to just one third (at the cost of some noticeable quality reduction)" вҖ” ModelPiper benchmarks put q4_0's perplexity increase around 7.6%, "comparable to the impact of going from Q8 to Q4 on model weights." Start with q8_0.
- `OLLAMA_CONTEXT_LENGTH=32768` вҖ” global default context; can be overridden per-model in a Modelfile or per-request with `num_ctx`.
- `OLLAMA_KEEP_ALIVE=-1` вҖ” keep the model resident (loads are slow for a 24GB model).
- `OLLAMA_NUM_PARALLEL=1` вҖ” critical: parallel slots multiply the KV cache (a 2K context Г— 4 parallel = 8K of allocation).

9.content:
Keep at 1 for a single-user coding agent so you don't waste RAM.
- Verify with `OLLAMA_DEBUG=1` вҖ” Ollama logs every variable and its resolved value at startup; if it shows your value, the setting is live, if it shows the default you set it in the wrong place. Windows logs are at `%LOCALAPPDATA%\Ollama\server.log`.

**GPU layer offload:** there is **no `OLLAMA_GPU_LAYERS`**; the real control is the model option `num_gpu` (a Modelfile `PARAMETER` or API option), which sets how many layers go to the GPU. `OLLAMA_NUM_GPU` is read by some builds but is unreliable (there are open bugs where it is ignored, e.g. issue #11437). The robust path is a Modelfile `PARAMETER num_gpu`. To reserve VRAM for the desktop, `OLLAMA_GPU_OVERHEAD=<bytes>` exists (e.g. ~1GB = `1073741824`).

**`OLLAMA_MAX_VRAM` confirmed non-functional** вҖ” removed/deprecated as "a hack"; use `OLLAMA_GPU_OVERHEAD` instead if you want to reserve VRAM. Do not use `OLLAMA_MAX_VRAM=64000000000`.

### 3. How MoE behaves with CPU offload

In a dense mod

10.content:
el every parameter is "active" each token, so offloading to RAM means slow PCIe/CPU work on every weight. In an MoE, the router fires only 8 of 256 experts (~3B of 35B params) per token. The "always active" parts (attention, shared/dense FFN, embeddings, norms) are small; the routed expert FFNs are the bulk of the size but are touched rarely. The optimal split: **always-active on GPU, routed experts in RAM.** As the "Understanding MoE Offloading" write-up explains: "Llama.cpp doesn't move the entire 24M parameter expert from RAM into VRAMвҖ”that would be way too slow. Instead, it sends the token's small activation vector from VRAM across the PCIe bus to system RAM. The CPU then performs the math using the expert weights residing in RAM, and the result is sent back across the PCIe bus." The real bottleneck is RAM bandwidth (DDR5 helps a lot), not raw CPU clock.

**llama.cpp flags (the canonical technique):** `-ngl 99 --n-cpu-moe N` keeps experts of the first N layers in CPU RAM; `-cmoe`/`--cpu-moe` keeps *all*

11.content:
experts on CPU; `-ot "\.ffn_.*_exps\.weight=CPU"` is the regex equivalent for a single GPU. These are llama.cpp/ik_llama.cpp options.

**Ollama vs llama.cpp:** Ollama does **not** expose `--n-cpu-moe` or `-ot`. Its newer engine performs MoE expert offload automatically when a model doesn't fit, and you bias it with `num_gpu`. This is less precise than llama.cpp. **Decision:** start with Ollama (simplest, your agent already targets it). If you can't get VRAM filled efficiently or want the last ~20% of speed, switch the model to **llama.cpp `llama-server`** (or llama-swap) where you control `--n-cpu-moe` exactly and point Larry at its OpenAI-compatible localhost endpoint.

**Real-world tok/s on 8GB + 64GB-class rigs (generation, smallвҶ’large context):**
- Measured `llama-bench` (GitHub Discussion #22183), unsloth Qwen3.6-35B-A3B UD-Q4_K_M, single GPU + system RAM, `-ngl 99 -fa 1 -ctk q8_0 -ctv q8_0 -ncmoe 11`: **35.20 Вұ 0.63 tok/s generation** (tg128), **268 tok/s prefill** (pp512).
- RTX 3070 Ti 8GB + 64GB

12.content:
RAM, Qwen3.6-35B-A3B, llama.cpp `-ngl 99 -ncmoe 99`: **33.5 tok/s**.
- RTX 3060 Ti 8GB + ~32GB, Qwen3-Coder-30B-A3B UD-Q4_K_XL, llama.cpp `--n-cpu-moe 40`: **32.49 tok/s** gen / ~52 tok/s prompt; at full 262K context ~**29 tok/s**. Going `--cpu-moe` (all experts on CPU) drops it to **13.4 tok/s** вҖ” showing tuning the offload number is what moves you from ~13 to ~32 tok/s.
- RTX 4060 Laptop 8GB + 32GB, Qwen3.6-35B-A3B Q4_K_M: self-reported **33вҖ“34 tok/s** warm decode (custom fork, treat cautiously).
- RTX 2060 6GB + 32GB DDR4 laptop, Qwen3.5-35B-A3B Q4_K_M, `-ngl 99 --n-cpu-moe 99 -ub 2048`: **~15 tok/s** gen at 102K context, 350вҖ“400 tok/s prefill.
- CPU-bound floor: Ryzen + 32GB, 4-bit Qwen3-Coder-30B-A3B: **12вҖ“15 tok/s**.

Your desktop RTX 4060 8GB + 64GB DDR5 should land in the **~30вҖ“35 tok/s** band at modest context вҖ” comparable to a dense 8B running fully on GPU, but with 35B-scale knowledge. By contrast, the dense Qwen3.6-27B at Q4 (~17GB) does NOT fit in 8GB and must spill ordinary layers to

13.content:
CPU, which is the slow case (dense offload can be 5вҖ“10Г— slower); expect well under 10 tok/s. This is the core reason to prefer the MoE.

### 4. Context length vs KV cache memory

The model is `qwen35moe`: 40 layers, key/value length 256, GQA. KV cache grows linearly with context, and at 32K+ it can exceed the size of the active weights. With **flash attention on + q8_0 KV cache**, you roughly halve that footprint. Practical guidance from the field:
- Qwen authors and the model card both advise keeping **вүҘ128K context to preserve thinking-mode capability** вҖ” but that is a quality-of-reasoning note, not a memory recommendation, and full 256K KV is large.
- For agentic coding you want enough context to hold several files + tool outputs while staying responsive. **32K (32768) is the recommended sweet spot.** It comfortably holds a working set of code, keeps prompt-processing snappy, and leaves RAM headroom. **64K** is a reasonable stretch for big multi-file tasks; **128K+** noticeably slows prompt process

14.content:
ing and eats RAM. Prompt processing (prefill) cost rises sharply with context вҖ” large `num_ctx` "just in case" is the most common self-inflicted slowdown.
- KV quantization tradeoff: `q8_0` is effectively free quality-wise and recommended; `q4_0` saves more (~7.6% perplexity hit) but degrades long-context quality and is more noticeable вҖ” only use it if you must push context and accept some loss. (KV quant is discouraged for embedding/vision-sensitive models, not relevant for text coding.)

### 5. Agentic coding / tool integration

Qwen3.6/3.5 has strong, native tool-calling, and unsloth specifically shipped tool-calling fixes (nested-object parsing) plus "Developer Role Support so Qwen3.6 can work in Codex, OpenCode and more." Ollama exposes tool calling through its `/api/chat` `tools` field and documents an agent loop. The uncensored variants preserve tool-calling вҖ” the HauhauCS card states "No changes to datasets or capabilities" and tool-calling is among the most quantization-resilient behaviors. The

15.content:
fredrezones55 build is tagged `tools` on Ollama.

**For reliable tool use:** use the Qwen chat template with `--jinja`-equivalent handling (Ollama applies the baked template; the HauhauCS Ollama build uses `RENDERER qwen3.5`/`PARSER qwen3.5`). Use the official Qwen coding sampler (per the Qwen model card, "Thinking mode for precise coding tasks (e.g. WebDev)"): **`temperature=0.6, top_p=0.95, top_k=20, min_p=0.0, presence_penalty=0.0, repetition_penalty=1.0`**, and "DO NOT use greedy decoding, as it can lead to performance degradation and endless repetitions." Keep `OLLAMA_NUM_PARALLEL=1` for deterministic single-agent behavior.

**Ollama agentic launchers** that exist for these models: `ollama launch claude`, `ollama launch codex` (and `codex-app`), `ollama launch opencode`, `ollama launch openclaw`, `ollama launch hermes`. For your local "Larry" agent + MCP, point it at the Ollama OpenAI-compatible endpoint (`http://localhost:11434`) or the llama.cpp `llama-server` endpoint if you switch. One field report

16.content:
flagged Hermes-agent deadlocks on Qwen3.6 needing `config.memory.nudge_interval=0` and `config.memory.flush_min_turns=0` вҖ” worth knowing if you use Hermes.

### 6. Concrete recommended configuration

**Step 1 вҖ” Pull the model (single line):**
```
ollama pull huihui_ai/Qwen3.6-abliterated:35b
```
(Alternative vision-patched uncensored build: `ollama pull fredrezones55/Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-Uncensored-HauhauCS-Aggressive:Q4`)

**Step 2 вҖ” Set Windows env vars (paste each line in CMD, one at a time, then fully quit + relaunch the Ollama tray app):**
```
setx OLLAMA_FLASH_ATTENTION 1
setx OLLAMA_KV_CACHE_TYPE q8_0
setx OLLAMA_KEEP_ALIVE -1
setx OLLAMA_MAX_LOADED_MODELS 1
setx OLLAMA_NUM_PARALLEL 1
```

**Step 3 вҖ” Create a custom Modelfile (save as `Modelfile.larry` in a folder, e.g. `C:\Users\<you>\larry`):**
```
FROM huihui_ai/Qwen3.6-abliterated:35b
PARAMETER num_ctx 32768
PARAMETER num_gpu 12
PARAMETER temperature 0.6
PARAMETER top_p 0.95
PARAMETER top_k 20
PARAMETER min_p 0
PARAMETER presence_penalty 0
PARAM

17.content:
ETER repeat_penalty 1.0
```

**Step 4 вҖ” Build the reusable model (single line):**
```
ollama create larry-qwen36 -f C:\Users\<you>\larry\Modelfile.larry
```

**Step 5 вҖ” Tune `num_gpu` against VRAM.** Start at **`num_gpu 12`** for an 8GB card with this 40-layer MoE (a conservative starting point that should leave headroom; this is the Ollama analogue of llama.cpp's "try `--n-cpu-moe` in the low 30s first, then adjust" guidance). Run the model, then in a second terminal watch VRAM:
```
nvidia-smi --query-gpu=memory.used,memory.free --format=csv -l 1
```
Also check Task Manager вҶ’ Performance вҶ’ GPU "Dedicated GPU memory." **On Windows, the driver will silently overflow past 8GB into shared RAM and run very slowly вҖ” so confirm GPU memory stays under ~7.3GB used** (leave ~0.7вҖ“1GB for the desktop). If you have headroom, rebuild with a higher `num_gpu` (e.g. 14, 16) to push more layers/experts onto the GPU and gain speed; if it overflows or OOMs, lower it. Re-run `ollama create` each time you change the v

18.content:
alue (the parameter is baked at build). Confirm the split with `ollama ps` (look for the CPU/GPU percentage).

**Recommended final settings:** Q4_K_M model, `num_ctx 32768`, flash attention on, `q8_0` KV cache, `num_gpu` tuned to fill ~7.3GB. **Expect ~30вҖ“35 tok/s** at small/medium context, tapering at very large context. If Ollama won't fill VRAM efficiently, switch to llama.cpp `llama-server` with `-ngl 99 --n-cpu-moe 34 -fa on -ctk q8_0 -ctv q8_0 -c 32768 --jinja` and tune the `--n-cpu-moe` number down to fill VRAM, pointing Larry at that endpoint.

## Recommendations
1. **Start now with `huihui_ai/Qwen3.6-abliterated:35b` (Q4_K_M, 24GB) on Ollama**, text-only, with the env vars and Modelfile above. This is the fastest path to a working uncensored agentic coder on your hardware.
2. **Tune `num_gpu` empirically** from 12 upward while watching `nvidia-smi` вҖ” the difference between a bad and good offload split is roughly 13 вҶ’ 32 tok/s. Stop when GPU memory used is ~7.3GB.
3. **Keep context at 32K** for

19.content:
daily agentic work; only raise to 64K for specific large-repo tasks. Never set it to 256K "just in case."
4. **If you need more control or speed, graduate to llama.cpp `llama-server`** with explicit `--n-cpu-moe`. This is the only way to get the exact expert-offload tuning the MoE rewards, and it's typically ~20вҖ“30% faster than Ollama on the same model.
5. **Benchmarks/thresholds that change the plan:** if you measure <15 tok/s, your offload split is wrong (too few GPU layers) or you're overflowing VRAM into shared RAM вҖ” fix `num_gpu`. If tool calls misformat, move from any Q3/Q2 quant up to Q4_K_M and confirm the Qwen template/parser is active. If you genuinely need vision, accept the rough mmproj path or use a dedicated VL model rather than burdening the coding agent.
6. **Dense 27B is a fallback, not the default:** it won't fit in 8GB and will run dense-offloaded (slow). Only prefer it if you observe the MoE giving inconsistent multi-step coherence вҖ” the dense model trades throughput for steadiness.

20.content:
## Caveats
- These uncensored/abliterated builds have significantly reduced safety filtering; the publishers explicitly warn outputs may be sensitive/inappropriate and place legal/ethical responsibility on the user.
- The fredrezones55/HauhauCS builds are community uploads with marketing-style claims ("0/465 Refusals," "best lossless uncensored"); the "33вҖ“34 tok/s on a 4060 Laptop" figure is self-reported via an undisclosed fork and should be treated cautiously. The measured 35.20 tok/s `llama-bench` run, the 3070 Ti 33.5 tok/s, and the 3060 Ti 32.5 tok/s numbers are better-sourced.
- Ollama's automatic MoE/layer scheduler has known bugs on some configs (e.g., misallocating MoE layers on partial offload, silently overflowing to host RAM вҖ” issue #14351). Always verify the actual split with `ollama ps` and VRAM tools rather than trusting that "it loaded."
- tok/s figures vary by Ollama/llama.cpp version, exact quant, BIOS/RAM speed, and background load; treat them as a band, not a guarantee. Your single-ch

21.content:
# Running Uncensored Qwen3.6-35B-A3B (and Dense Qwen3.6-27B) via Ollama on Windows for Agentic Coding вҖ” Tuned Setup Plan

## TL;DR
- **Pull the MoE model `huihui_ai/Qwen3.6-abliterated:35b` (24GB, Q4_K_M, text+tools, tool-calling intact) as your primary agentic-coding driver**, or the vision-patched `fredrezones55/Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-Uncensored-HauhauCS-Aggressive:Q4` (22GB); keep the dense `huihui_ai/Qwen3.6-abliterated:27b` (17GB) as a steadier-but-slower fallback. On your RTX 4060 8GB + 64GB DDR5 rig, the 35B-A3B MoE is the right choice precisely because only ~3B of 35B params are active per token, so offloading its experts to RAM costs far less speed than offloading a dense model would.
- **Expect ~30вҖ“35 tok/s generation at modest context**, dropping toward ~15вҖ“29 tok/s at very large context вҖ” corroborated across multiple 8GB-VRAM + 64GB-RAM rigs (and a measured `llama-bench` run on Qwen3.6-35B-A3B UD-Q4_K_M at **35.20 tok/s gen / 268 tok/s prefill**). Set these Windows user environment variables (via

22.content:
System Properties вҶ’ Environment Variables or `setx`), then **fully restart the Ollama app**: `OLLAMA_FLASH_ATTENTION=1`, `OLLAMA_KV_CACHE_TYPE=q8_0`, `OLLAMA_CONTEXT_LENGTH=32768`, `OLLAMA_KEEP_ALIVE=-1`. `OLLAMA_MAX_VRAM` is **not** a working variable вҖ” ignore that advice.
- **Use a custom Modelfile** baking in `PARAMETER num_ctx 32768`, `PARAMETER num_gpu <tuned>`, and the Qwen coding sampler settings, then tune `num_gpu` down from a starting guess while watching VRAM in Task Manager / `nvidia-smi`. The sweet spot for agentic coding is **32K context** (q8_0 KV cache + flash attention); go to 64K only if a task needs it.

## Key Findings

**1. The model is real, current, and tool-capable.** `fredrezones55/Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-Uncensored-HauhauCS-Aggressive` exists on ollama.com, was updated ~6 days ago, has ~10.9K downloads, advertises "0/465 Refusals," and is tagged `vision`, `tools`, `thinking` with a 256K context window. The base is Qwen/Qwen3.6-35B-A3B: 35B total / ~3B active, 256 experts with 8 routed p

23.content:
er token, 40 layers, hybrid linear+full attention (3:1), 262,144 native context. The note "even tool calling at 2-bit quant is strong" traces to the unsloth GGUF discussion; independent quant testing finds tool-calling among the most quantization-resilient categories (KL divergence ~0.069вҖ“0.078 at Q8_0).

**2. Two uncensored families to choose from.** The HauhauCS/fredrezones55 builds are imatrix-quantized and vision-patched. The huihui_ai abliterated builds are a cleaner, more conventional choice for a coding agent: `huihui_ai/Qwen3.6-abliterated:35b` (24GB, the MoE) and `:27b` (17GB, the dense model, Q4_K_M, arch `qwen35`, 27.8B params).

**3. MoE + CPU offload is the whole trick.** With only ~3B active params per token, the optimal strategy is to keep attention/shared/KV on the GPU and push the big, rarely-active expert FFN tensors to system RAM. In llama.cpp this is `-ngl 99 --n-cpu-moe N` (or `-ot "ffn_*_exps=CPU"`). Ollama does not expose `--n-cpu-moe`; it does this automatically via its scheduler, an

24.content:
d you steer it with `num_gpu`.

**4. `OLLAMA_MAX_VRAM` is dead.** Per GitHub issue #5754 ("OLLAMA_MAX_VRAM is ignored"): "I set the OLLAMA_MAX_VRAM env var. I see it is correctly parsed in the logs, but the limit itself is ignored... Even when I set it to an absurdly low value like 5 it still uses more than 6GB." It was deprecated because "it was a hack" and `OLLAMA_GPU_OVERHEAD` "should be used instead." Setting `OLLAMA_MAX_VRAM=64000000000` does nothing. There is no `/etc/ollama/ollama.env` and no `systemctl` on Windows вҖ” those are Linux-only.

28.content:
V cache quantization. Confirmed in Ollama's source (`envconfig`).
- `OLLAMA_KV_CACHE_TYPE=q8_0` вҖ” halves KV memory vs f16 with negligible quality loss. Per Sam McLeod (smcleod.net, who wrote the Ollama KV-quant PR): "Q8_0 вҖ” Minimal quality impact for normal text generation models... Perplexity measurements on an early implementation showed it added around 0.002-0.05~ perplexity." Only takes effect with flash attention on. `q4_0` "cuts it down to just one third (at the cost of some noticeable quality reduction)" вҖ” ModelPiper benchmarks put q4_0's perplexity increase around 7.6%, "comparable to the impact of going from Q8 to Q4 on model weights." Start with q8_0.
- `OLLAMA_CONTEXT_LENGTH=32768` вҖ” global default context; can be overridden per-model in a Modelfile or per-request with `num_ctx`.
- `OLLAMA_KEEP_ALIVE=-1` вҖ” keep the model resident (loads are slow for a 24GB model).
- `OLLAMA_NUM_PARALLEL=1` вҖ” critical: parallel slots multiply the KV cache (a 2K context Г— 4 parallel = 8K of allocation).

29.content:
Keep at 1 for a single-user coding agent so you don't waste RAM.
- Verify with `OLLAMA_DEBUG=1` вҖ” Ollama logs every variable and its resolved value at startup; if it shows your value, the setting is live, if it shows the default you set it in the wrong place. Windows logs are at `%LOCALAPPDATA%\Ollama\server.log`.

**GPU layer offload:** there is **no `OLLAMA_GPU_LAYERS`**; the real control is the model option `num_gpu` (a Modelfile `PARAMETER` or API option), which sets how many layers go to the GPU. `OLLAMA_NUM_GPU` is read by some builds but is unreliable (there are open bugs where it is ignored, e.g. issue #11437). The robust path is a Modelfile `PARAMETER num_gpu`. To reserve VRAM for the desktop, `OLLAMA_GPU_OVERHEAD=<bytes>` exists (e.g. ~1GB = `1073741824`).

**`OLLAMA_MAX_VRAM` confirmed non-functional** вҖ” removed/deprecated as "a hack"; use `OLLAMA_GPU_OVERHEAD` instead if you want to reserve VRAM. Do not use `OLLAMA_MAX_VRAM=64000000000`.

### 3. How MoE behaves with CPU offload

In a dense mod

30.content:
el every parameter is "active" each token, so offloading to RAM means slow PCIe/CPU work on every weight. In an MoE, the router fires only 8 of 256 experts (~3B of 35B params) per token. The "always active" parts (attention, shared/dense FFN, embeddings, norms) are small; the routed expert FFNs are the bulk of the size but are touched rarely. The optimal split: **always-active on GPU, routed experts in RAM.** As the "Understanding MoE Offloading" write-up explains: "Llama.cpp doesn't move the entire 24M parameter expert from RAM into VRAMвҖ”that would be way too slow. Instead, it sends the token's small activation vector from VRAM across the PCIe bus to system RAM. The CPU then performs the math using the expert weights residing in RAM, and the result is sent back across the PCIe bus." The real bottleneck is RAM bandwidth (DDR5 helps a lot), not raw CPU clock.

**llama.cpp flags (the canonical technique):** `-ngl 99 --n-cpu-moe N` keeps experts of the first N layers in CPU RAM; `-cmoe`/`--cpu-moe` keeps *all*

31.content:
experts on CPU; `-ot "\.ffn_.*_exps\.weight=CPU"` is the regex equivalent for a single GPU. These are llama.cpp/ik_llama.cpp options.

**Ollama vs llama.cpp:** Ollama does **not** expose `--n-cpu-moe` or `-ot`. Its newer engine performs MoE expert offload automatically when a model doesn't fit, and you bias it with `num_gpu`. This is less precise than llama.cpp. **Decision:** start with Ollama (simplest, your agent already targets it). If you can't get VRAM filled efficiently or want the last ~20% of speed, switch the model to **llama.cpp `llama-server`** (or llama-swap) where you control `--n-cpu-moe` exactly and point Larry at its OpenAI-compatible localhost endpoint.

**Real-world tok/s on 8GB + 64GB-class rigs (generation, smallвҶ’large context):**
- Measured `llama-bench` (GitHub Discussion #22183), unsloth Qwen3.6-35B-A3B UD-Q4_K_M, single GPU + system RAM, `-ngl 99 -fa 1 -ctk q8_0 -ctv q8_0 -ncmoe 11`: **35.20 Вұ 0.63 tok/s generation** (tg128), **268 tok/s prefill** (pp512).
- RTX 3070 Ti 8GB + 64GB

32.content:
RAM, Qwen3.6-35B-A3B, llama.cpp `-ngl 99 -ncmoe 99`: **33.5 tok/s**.
- RTX 3060 Ti 8GB + ~32GB, Qwen3-Coder-30B-A3B UD-Q4_K_XL, llama.cpp `--n-cpu-moe 40`: **32.49 tok/s** gen / ~52 tok/s prompt; at full 262K context ~**29 tok/s**. Going `--cpu-moe` (all experts on CPU) drops it to **13.4 tok/s** вҖ” showing tuning the offload number is what moves you from ~13 to ~32 tok/s.
- RTX 4060 Laptop 8GB + 32GB, Qwen3.6-35B-A3B Q4_K_M: self-reported **33вҖ“34 tok/s** warm decode (custom fork, treat cautiously).
- RTX 2060 6GB + 32GB DDR4 laptop, Qwen3.5-35B-A3B Q4_K_M, `-ngl 99 --n-cpu-moe 99 -ub 2048`: **~15 tok/s** gen at 102K context, 350вҖ“400 tok/s prefill.
- CPU-bound floor: Ryzen + 32GB, 4-bit Qwen3-Coder-30B-A3B: **12вҖ“15 tok/s**.

Your desktop RTX 4060 8GB + 64GB DDR5 should land in the **~30вҖ“35 tok/s** band at modest context вҖ” comparable to a dense 8B running fully on GPU, but with 35B-scale knowledge. By contrast, the dense Qwen3.6-27B at Q4 (~17GB) does NOT fit in 8GB and must spill ordinary layers to

33.content:
CPU, which is the slow case (dense offload can be 5вҖ“10Г— slower); expect well under 10 tok/s. This is the core reason to prefer the MoE.

### 4. Context length vs KV cache memory

The model is `qwen35moe`: 40 layers, key/value length 256, GQA. KV cache grows linearly with context, and at 32K+ it can exceed the size of the active weights. With **flash attention on + q8_0 KV cache**, you roughly halve that footprint. Practical guidance from the field:
- Qwen authors and the model card both advise keeping **вүҘ128K context to preserve thinking-mode capability** вҖ” but that is a quality-of-reasoning note, not a memory recommendation, and full 256K KV is large.
- For agentic coding you want enough context to hold several files + tool outputs while staying responsive. **32K (32768) is the recommended sweet spot.** It comfortably holds a working set of code, keeps prompt-processing snappy, and leaves RAM headroom. **64K** is a reasonable stretch for big multi-file tasks; **128K+** noticeably slows prompt process

34.content:
ing and eats RAM. Prompt processing (prefill) cost rises sharply with context вҖ” large `num_ctx` "just in case" is the most common self-inflicted slowdown.
- KV quantization tradeoff: `q8_0` is effectively free quality-wise and recommended; `q4_0` saves more (~7.6% perplexity hit) but degrades long-context quality and is more noticeable вҖ” only use it if you must push context and accept some loss. (KV quant is discouraged for embedding/vision-sensitive models, not relevant for text coding.)

### 5. Agentic coding / tool integration

Qwen3.6/3.5 has strong, native tool-calling, and unsloth specifically shipped tool-calling fixes (nested-object parsing) plus "Developer Role Support so Qwen3.6 can work in Codex, OpenCode and more." Ollama exposes tool calling through its `/api/chat` `tools` field and documents an agent loop. The uncensored variants preserve tool-calling вҖ” the HauhauCS card states "No changes to datasets or capabilities" and tool-calling is among the most quantization-resilient behaviors. The

35.content:
fredrezones55 build is tagged `tools` on Ollama.

**For reliable tool use:** use the Qwen chat template with `--jinja`-equivalent handling (Ollama applies the baked template; the HauhauCS Ollama build uses `RENDERER qwen3.5`/`PARSER qwen3.5`). Use the official Qwen coding sampler (per the Qwen model card, "Thinking mode for precise coding tasks (e.g. WebDev)"): **`temperature=0.6, top_p=0.95, top_k=20, min_p=0.0, presence_penalty=0.0, repetition_penalty=1.0`**, and "DO NOT use greedy decoding, as it can lead to performance degradation and endless repetitions." Keep `OLLAMA_NUM_PARALLEL=1` for deterministic single-agent behavior.

**Ollama agentic launchers** that exist for these models: `ollama launch claude`, `ollama launch codex` (and `codex-app`), `ollama launch opencode`, `ollama launch openclaw`, `ollama launch hermes`. For your local "Larry" agent + MCP, point it at the Ollama OpenAI-compatible endpoint (`http://localhost:11434`) or the llama.cpp `llama-server` endpoint if you switch. One field report

36.content:
flagged Hermes-agent deadlocks on Qwen3.6 needing `config.memory.nudge_interval=0` and `config.memory.flush_min_turns=0` вҖ” worth knowing if you use Hermes.

### 6. Concrete recommended configuration

**Step 1 вҖ” Pull the model (single line):**
```
ollama pull huihui_ai/Qwen3.6-abliterated:35b
```
(Alternative vision-patched uncensored build: `ollama pull fredrezones55/Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-Uncensored-HauhauCS-Aggressive:Q4`)

**Step 2 вҖ” Set Windows env vars (paste each line in CMD, one at a time, then fully quit + relaunch the Ollama tray app):**
```
setx OLLAMA_FLASH_ATTENTION 1
setx OLLAMA_KV_CACHE_TYPE q8_0
setx OLLAMA_KEEP_ALIVE -1
setx OLLAMA_MAX_LOADED_MODELS 1
setx OLLAMA_NUM_PARALLEL 1
```

**Step 3 вҖ” Create a custom Modelfile (save as `Modelfile.larry` in a folder, e.g. `C:\Users\<you>\larry`):**
```
FROM huihui_ai/Qwen3.6-abliterated:35b
PARAMETER num_ctx 32768
PARAMETER num_gpu 12
PARAMETER temperature 0.6
PARAMETER top_p 0.95
PARAMETER top_k 20
PARAMETER min_p 0
PARAMETER presence_penalty 0
PARAM

37.content:
ETER repeat_penalty 1.0
```

**Step 4 вҖ” Build the reusable model (single line):**
```
ollama create larry-qwen36 -f C:\Users\<you>\larry\Modelfile.larry
```

**Step 5 вҖ” Tune `num_gpu` against VRAM.** Start at **`num_gpu 12`** for an 8GB card with this 40-layer MoE (a conservative starting point that should leave headroom; this is the Ollama analogue of llama.cpp's "try `--n-cpu-moe` in the low 30s first, then adjust" guidance). Run the model, then in a second terminal watch VRAM:
```
nvidia-smi --query-gpu=memory.used,memory.free --format=csv -l 1
```
Also check Task Manager вҶ’ Performance вҶ’ GPU "Dedicated GPU memory." **On Windows, the driver will silently overflow past 8GB into shared RAM and run very slowly вҖ” so confirm GPU memory stays under ~7.3GB used** (leave ~0.7вҖ“1GB for the desktop). If you have headroom, rebuild with a higher `num_gpu` (e.g. 14, 16) to push more layers/experts onto the GPU and gain speed; if it overflows or OOMs, lower it. Re-run `ollama create` each time you change the v

38.content:
alue (the parameter is baked at build). Confirm the split with `ollama ps` (look for the CPU/GPU percentage).

**Recommended final settings:** Q4_K_M model, `num_ctx 32768`, flash attention on, `q8_0` KV cache, `num_gpu` tuned to fill ~7.3GB. **Expect ~30вҖ“35 tok/s** at small/medium context, tapering at very large context. If Ollama won't fill VRAM efficiently, switch to llama.cpp `llama-server` with `-ngl 99 --n-cpu-moe 34 -fa on -ctk q8_0 -ctv q8_0 -c 32768 --jinja` and tune the `--n-cpu-moe` number down to fill VRAM, pointing Larry at that endpoint.

## Recommendations
1. **Start now with `huihui_ai/Qwen3.6-abliterated:35b` (Q4_K_M, 24GB) on Ollama**, text-only, with the env vars and Modelfile above. This is the fastest path to a working uncensored agentic coder on your hardware.
2. **Tune `num_gpu` empirically** from 12 upward while watching `nvidia-smi` вҖ” the difference between a bad and good offload split is roughly 13 вҶ’ 32 tok/s. Stop when GPU memory used is ~7.3GB.
3. **Keep context at 32K** for

39.content:
daily agentic work; only raise to 64K for specific large-repo tasks. Never set it to 256K "just in case."
4. **If you need more control or speed, graduate to llama.cpp `llama-server`** with explicit `--n-cpu-moe`. This is the only way to get the exact expert-offload tuning the MoE rewards, and it's typically ~20вҖ“30% faster than Ollama on the same model.
5. **Benchmarks/thresholds that change the plan:** if you measure <15 tok/s, your offload split is wrong (too few GPU layers) or you're overflowing VRAM into shared RAM вҖ” fix `num_gpu`. If tool calls misformat, move from any Q3/Q2 quant up to Q4_K_M and confirm the Qwen template/parser is active. If you genuinely need vision, accept the rough mmproj path or use a dedicated VL model rather than burdening the coding agent.
6. **Dense 27B is a fallback, not the default:** it won't fit in 8GB and will run dense-offloaded (slow). Only prefer it if you observe the MoE giving inconsistent multi-step coherence вҖ” the dense model trades throughput for steadiness.

40.content:
## Caveats
- These uncensored/abliterated builds have significantly reduced safety filtering; the publishers explicitly warn outputs may be sensitive/inappropriate and place legal/ethical responsibility on the user.
- The fredrezones55/HauhauCS builds are community uploads with marketing-style claims ("0/465 Refusals," "best lossless uncensored"); the "33вҖ“34 tok/s on a 4060 Laptop" figure is self-reported via an undisclosed fork and should be treated cautiously. The measured 35.20 tok/s `llama-bench` run, the 3070 Ti 33.5 tok/s, and the 3060 Ti 32.5 tok/s numbers are better-sourced.
- Ollama's automatic MoE/layer scheduler has known bugs on some configs (e.g., misallocating MoE layers on partial offload, silently overflowing to host RAM вҖ” issue #14351). Always verify the actual split with `ollama ps` and VRAM tools rather than trusting that "it loaded."
- tok/s figures vary by Ollama/llama.cpp version, exact quant, BIOS/RAM speed, and background load; treat them as a band, not a guarantee. Your single-ch

This script creates a ReAct agent using Ollama's local LLM and integrates it with LangGraph. It demonstrates how to:
1. Initialize Ollama client pointing to `http://localhost:11434/v1`
2. Define tools for web search, file search, and getting time
3. Create a graph with agent and tool nodes
4. Set up the workflow to loop until the agent stops calling functions
5. Run the agent with a sample query

The agent will continue calling functions until it determines it has completed its task. The loop behavior is handled by LangGraph's streaming int
